title: How to Track Calories Correctly (So the Numbers Actually Match Your Results)
introduction: Calorie tracking can feel confusing at first, but it is one of the most reliable ways to understand why your weight is or is not changing. The problem is that many people track loosely or inconsistently, then assume calorie counting "doesn’t work". In reality, a few simple techniques can make your logging accurate enough that your results line up with your expectations.

sections.heading: Why Small Tracking Errors Lead to Big Frustration
sections.content:
If your logging is off by 150–300 calories per day, that is enough to completely erase a moderate deficit. The tricky part is that these errors often come from innocent places: estimating portions by eye, forgetting cooking oils, or not logging small snacks.

Over a week, that margin of error can reach 1000–2000 calories—roughly the difference between losing weight and staying stuck. When you think you are at 1600 calories but are actually at 2000, it is easy to blame your metabolism instead of your methods.

The solution is not to obsess over every bite forever, but to tighten your tracking for a few weeks so you can build a realistic picture of your intake. Once you understand your patterns, you can loosen up while still keeping results on track.

sections.heading: Start With Realistic Maintenance and a Clear Target
sections.content:
Before you worry about perfect tracking, you need a reasonable calorie target. You can estimate your maintenance calories using a TDEE calculator or by logging everything you eat for 1–2 weeks while keeping your weight roughly stable.

Once you know your approximate maintenance, choose a deficit of 300–500 calories per day for steady, sustainable fat loss. For example, if maintenance is 2300, aim for 1800–2000 calories. This range is large enough to give you results but not so aggressive that you feel miserable.

Eati helps here by converting plain‑language meal descriptions into calorie estimates, so you do not have to manually look up every food.

sections.heading: Weigh and Measure Key Foods (At Least for a While)
sections.content:
You do not need to weigh every ingredient forever, but it is incredibly eye‑opening to measure the foods you eat most often. Many people discover that their "tablespoon" of peanut butter is closer to two, or that their bowl of cereal is really three servings.

Focus your measuring effort on calorie‑dense foods: oils, nut butters, cheese, dressings, snacks, and desserts. For lean proteins and starchy carbs, weighing a typical portion a few times helps you build better portion awareness.

Once you understand the true size of your usual servings, you can go back to estimating by eye with much better accuracy. Use Eati to log both your weighed meals and your estimated ones so you can see how the numbers compare.

sections.heading: Do Not Forget Oils, Drinks, and Extras
sections.content:
Some of the most commonly missed calories come from:

• Cooking oils and butter used in pans
• Salad dressings and sauces
• Sugary drinks, creamers, and alcohol
• "Tastes" while cooking or finishing kids’ leftovers

Individually, each one might seem small, but together they can easily add 300–600 calories per day. For at least two weeks, commit to logging them. You may be surprised by how much they contribute to your daily total.

If you use Eati, include these details in your meal descriptions (for example, "grilled chicken with 1 tbsp olive oil" instead of just "grilled chicken"). The more specific you are, the more useful your data will be.

sections.heading: Track Weekends and Social Meals Honestly
sections.content:
Many people are meticulous from Monday to Thursday, then relax their tracking when eating out or socializing. This is understandable—but it can hide the real reason progress is slow.

You do not have to hit your exact target at every social event, but try to log your best estimate. Describe the meal in Eati as accurately as you can: "2 slices pepperoni pizza, side salad with dressing, 2 beers" is far better than skipping the log entirely.

When you look back over several weeks, you will be able to see whether weekends are consistently bumping your average calories above your fat‑loss range. If they are, you can make small adjustments without needing a completely different plan.

sections.heading: Use Data to Adjust, Not to Judge Yourself
sections.content:
Accurate calorie tracking is a feedback tool, not a moral scorecard. The goal is not to prove that you are "good" or "bad", but to understand how your habits affect your body.

After 2–4 weeks of honest logging and consistent weigh‑ins, compare your average calorie intake with your weight trend. If you are not losing at the expected rate, adjust your target or tighten up obvious weak spots (like unlogged snacks or weekend meals).

Eati makes this process easier by handling the heavy lifting of calorie and macro estimation. You bring the honesty; the app shows you the patterns. Together, that is enough to make fat loss predictable instead of confusing.

midArticleCta: Ready to see what you are really eating? Describe your next few meals in Eati and let the app calculate calories and macros so you can align your intake with your goals.
conclusion: Tracking calories correctly does not require perfection, but it does require consistency, honesty, and a few simple habits. By measuring key foods, logging oils and extras, tracking weekends, and reviewing your data calmly, you turn calorie counting into a powerful, low‑stress tool. Combine these strategies with Eati’s fast, text‑based logging and you will finally understand how your daily choices translate into weekly results on the scale—and how to tweak them for steady, sustainable fat loss.
